elements.sent-2411-001: On a class of dynamical spacetimes which are asymptotic as $t\to\infty$ to a stationary spacetime containing a horizon $\cH_0$, we show the existence of a unique null hypersurface $\cH$ which is asymptotic to $\cH_0$.
elements.sent-2411-002: This is a special case of a general unstable manifold theorem for perturbations of flows which translate in time and have a normal sink at an invariant manifold in space.
elements.sent-2411-003: Examples of horizons $\cH_0$ to which our result applies include event horizons of subextremal Kerr and Kerr--Newman black holes as well as event and cosmological horizons of subextremal Kerr--Newman--de~Sitter black holes.
elements.sent-2411-004: In the Kerr(--Newman) case, we show that $\cH$ is equal to the boundary of the black hole region of the dynamical spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this note we give a short proof of (a generalization of) the recent result by Chen--Klainerman \cite{ChenKlainermanHorizon on the regularity of the future event horizon of perturbations of subextremal Kerr black holes.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Underlying our proof is a general unstable manifold theorem based on \cite{HintzPolytrap .}
elements.sent-2411-007: A simple illustrative example, which is a toy model for the time $1$ null-geodesic flow near the event horizon of Kerr in phase space, is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-008: Let $a\in\R$ and consider \begin{equation}\label{EqIEx} f_0(t,\phi,z) = (t-1,\phi+a-z,z/2),\quad t\in\R,\ \phi\in\Sph^1=\R/2\pi\Z,\ z\in(-1,1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-010: Then we prove that for any perturbation $f(t,\phi,z)=f_0(t,\phi,z)+(0,\cO(t^{-\alpha ),\cO(t^{-\alpha ))$, $\alpha>0$, there exist $t_0\geq 1$ and a unique smooth submanifold $\cH\subset[t_0,\infty)\times\Sph^1\times(-1,1)$ which is $\cO(t^{-\alpha )$-close to $\cH_0$ and so that $f(\cH)\cap\{t\geq t_0\}\subset\cH$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: We set up and prove our general unstable manifold type theorem in~\S\ref{SU .}
elements.sent-2411-012: In~\S\ref{SH , we put the construction of perturbed horizons into this setup.}
elements.sent-2411-013: In essence, we construct a smooth submanifold $\Gamma^{\rm u}$ of phase space which asymptotes at late times to the conormal bundle $\Gamma_0$ (projected to the cosphere bundle) of the unperturbed event horizon.
elements.sent-2411-014: The key dynamical input is that the past geodesic flow on subextremal Kerr (and its generalizations), lifted to phase space, is a sink in directions normal to $\Gamma_0$.
elements.sent-2411-015: We then show that $\Gamma^{\rm u}$ projects to the base as (and is the conormal bundle of) a smooth null hypersurface (Theorem~\ref{ThmHPert ).}
elements.sent-2411-016: Under mild global assumptions on the perturbed Kerr spacetime, we show that this hypersurface coincides with the event horizon (Theorem~\ref{ThmHCI ).}
elements.sent-2411-017: Let $\cX$ be a closed manifold and $\Gamma\subset\cX$ a smooth closed submanifold (in particular compact).
elements.sent-2411-018: Let $\bar f\colon\cX\to\cX$ be a smooth map with $\bar f(\Gamma)=\Gamma$ for which there exists an open neighborhood $\cU\supset\Gamma$ so that $\bar f|_\cU\colon\cU\to\bar f(\cU)$ is a diffeomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-019: Since the differential $D\bar f\colon T_\Gamma\cX\to T_\Gamma\cX$ restricts to a vector bundle map \begin{equation}\label{EqIDf} \Gamma_p\bar f:=D_p\bar f|_{T_p\Gamma}\colon T_p\Gamma\to T_{\bar f(p)}\Gamma,\end{equation} it also induces a map on the normal bundle $N\Gamma=T_\Gamma\cX/T\Gamma$ which we denote \begin{equation}\label{EqIDf2} N_p\bar f \colon N_p\Gamma \to N_{\bar f(p)}\Gamma.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-020: Suppose that $\bar f|_\Gamma$ is an almost-isometry and $\Gamma$ is a normal sink for $\bar f$ in the following sense: there exist a fiber metric on $N\Gamma$ and, for all $\eps>0$, a fiber metric on $T\Gamma$ so that \begin{equation}\label{EqINormHyp} \|N_p\bar f\| < 1;\qquad 1-\eps<m(\Gamma_p\bar f):=\inf_{w\in T_p\Gamma,\,\|w\|=1} \|\Gamma_p\bar f(w)\|\leq\|\Gamma_p\bar f\|<1+\eps.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-021: Define the map \begin{equation}\label{EqIf0} f_0 \colon \cM:=\R\times\cX\to\cM,\quad f_0(t,x) = (t-1,\bar f(x)),\end{equation} which maps $\Gamma_0:=\R\times\Gamma$ to itself.
elements.sent-2411-022: We measure the size of perturbations of $f_0$ using:.
elements.sent-2411-024: For $m\in\N_0\cup\{\infty\}$, write $\cC_b^m(\cM)$ for the space of $u\in\CI(\cM)$ so that all of its up to $m$ many derivatives along $\pa_t$ and smooth vector fields on $\cX$ are bounded.
elements.sent-2411-025: For a bundle $E\to\cX$, the space $\cC_b^\infty(\cM;E)$ is defined analogously using local trivializations of $E$.. Furthermore $\rho\cC_b^m(\cM)=\{u\colon\rho^{-1 u\in\cC_b^m(\cM)\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Fix a Riemannian metric on $\cX$ and suppose $f\colon\cM\cap\{t\geq t_0\}\to\cM$, is a shift by $-1$ in $t$ and a $\rho\cC_b^\infty$ perturbation of $\bar f$ in space.. That is, \begin{equation}\label{EqIf} f(t,x)=\bigl(t-1,\exp_{\bar f(x)}\tilde V(t,\bar f(x))\bigr),\quad \tilde V(t,\cdot)\in\cV(\cX)=\CI(\cX;T\cX),\ \tilde V\in\rho\CI_b(\cM;T\cX).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{EqIf} f(t,x)=\bigl(t-1,\exp_{\bar f(x)}\tilde V(t,\bar f(x))\bigr),\quad \tilde V(t,\cdot)\in\cV(\cX)=\CI(\cX;T\cX),\ \tilde V\in\rho\CI_b(\cM;T\cX).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-028: Moreover, $\Gamma^{\rm u}$ is the unique $f$-invariant submanifold within the class of manifolds approaching $\Gamma_0$ in a $\rho\cC_b^1$ sense.
elements.sent-2411-029: Finally, given any $\tilde\Gamma^{\rm u}$ which approaches $\Gamma_0$ in a $\tilde\rho\cC_b^1$ sense for some weight function $\tilde\rho$, one obtains $\Gamma^{\rm u}$ as the $\cC_b^0$ limit of iterates $\tilde\Gamma^{\rm u _0=\tilde\Gamma^{\rm u}\cap\{t\geq t_0\}$, $\tilde\Gamma^{\rm u _{n+1 =f(\tilde\Gamma^{\rm u _n)\cap\{t\geq t_0\}$.. \end{thm.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Fix any complementary subbundle $S\to\Gamma$ of $T_\Gamma\cX$ to $T\Gamma$; thus $T_\Gamma\cX\to N\Gamma$ induces \begin{equation}\label{EqRMainIso} S\cong N\Gamma.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-031: We fix a fiber inner product on $S$ by pulling back the fiber inner product on $N\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-032: If $S$ is invariant under $D\bar f$, i.e.\ $D\bar f\colon S_p\to S_{\bar f(p)}$, then~\eqref{EqINormHyp implies the $r$-normal hyperbolicity (for every $r$) of $\bar f$ at $\Gamma$, with trivial unstable normal bundle: in the notation of \cite[\S{2.3 ]{HintzPolytrap , one can take $\bar N^u=0$ (trivial bundle over $\Gamma$) and $\bar N^s=S$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Since then all assumptions of \cite[Theorem~2.3]{HintzPolytrap are satisfied, we obtain the existence and uniqueness of $\Gamma^{\rm u}$ as claimed.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The final statement follows immediately from the proof of \cite[Theorem~2.3]{HintzPolytrap which, following \cite{HirschPughShubInvariantManifolds , is based on a graph transform argument {\`a la Hadamard.}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: See in particular \cite[equation~(2.22)]{HintzPolytrap where the constant denoted $C_\Sigma$ there can be taken arbitrary large but fixed (cf.\ \cite[equation~(2.36)]{HintzPolytrap ).}}
elements.sent-2411-036: To complete the proof, it thus suffices to show that the assumptions~\eqref{EqIDf --\eqref{EqINormHyp imply the existence of a smooth $D\bar f$-invariant complementary subbundle $\bar N^s\subset T_\Gamma\cX$.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: We work in the splitting \begin{equation}\label{EqISplit} T_\Gamma\cX=T\Gamma\oplus S.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-038: Write $\pi_S\colon T_\Gamma\cX\to S$ for the projection, and $S_p\bar f=\pi_S\circ D_p\bar f|_{S_p}\colon S_p\to S_{\bar f(p)}$ for $N_p f$ under the identification~\eqref{EqRMainIso .}
elements.sent-2411-039: The fiber over a point $p\in\Gamma$ of any complementary bundle can be written as \begin{equation}\label{EqINsFiber} \bar N^{\rm s}_p = \{(A_p(w),w)\colon w\in S_p\}\end{equation} for some $A_p\in\Hom(S_p,T_p\Gamma)$.
elements.sent-2411-040: We thus need to find \begin{equation}A\in\Hom(S,T\Gamma)\ \ s.t.\ \ D_p\bar f\bigl(A_p(w),w\bigr) = \bigl(A_{\bar f(p)}(w'),w'\bigr)\quad \forall\,p\in\Gamma,\ w\in S_p.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}A\in\Hom(S,T\Gamma)\ \ s.t.\ \ D_p\bar f\bigl(A_p(w),w\bigr) = \bigl(A_{\bar f(p)}(w'),w'\bigr)\quad \forall\,p\in\Gamma,\ w\in S_p.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-041: The desired $A$ is thus an invariant section for the map $\ell\colon A\mapsto A'$ defined by \begin{equation}\label{EqIellp} D_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f\bigl(A'_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}(w),w\bigr) = \bigl(A_p(w'),w'\bigr).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-042: Define $\Gamma S\bar f$ by writing \begin{equation}D_p\bar f =  \Gamma_p\bar f \Gamma S_p\bar f 0 S_p\bar f \end{equation} in the splitting~\eqref{EqISplit .}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}D_p\bar f =  \Gamma_p\bar f \Gamma S_p\bar f 0 S_p\bar f \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-043: In~\eqref{EqIellp , we must then have $w'=S_{\bar f^{-1 (p)}\bar f(w)$, and therefore \begin{equation}\label{EqIEllp2} A'_{\bar f^{-1}(p)} = (\Gamma_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f)^{-1} \circ\bigl( A_p \circ S_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f - \Gamma S_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f\bigr).\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\label{EqIEllp2} A'_{\bar f^{-1}(p)} = (\Gamma_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f)^{-1} \circ\bigl( A_p \circ S_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f - \Gamma S_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f\bigr).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-044: Note that $\ell\colon A\mapsto A'$ is a fiber bundle map (in fact, affine linear) on the bundle $\Hom(S,T\Gamma)\to\Gamma$ covering $\bar f^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-045: We estimate the operator norm $k_p$ of the linearization of $\ell_p\colon A_p\mapsto A'_{\bar f^{-1 (p)}$ (which maps $B\mapsto(\Gamma_{\bar f^{-1 (p)}\bar f)^{-1}\circ B\circ S_{\bar f^{-1 (p)}\bar f$) by \begin{equation}k_p \leq \| (\Gamma_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f)^{-1} \| \|S_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f \| = m(\Gamma_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f)^{-1} \| S_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f \|.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}k_p \leq \| (\Gamma_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f)^{-1} \| \|S_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f \| = m(\Gamma_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f)^{-1} \| S_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f \|.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-046: For every $r\in\N$ we can choose the fiber metric on $T\Gamma$ so that $\alpha_p:=\|\Gamma_{\bar f^{-1 (p)}\bar f\|<1+\eps$ for any fixed $\eps>0$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: By~\eqref{EqINormHyp , this implies \begin{equation}k_p\alpha_p^{r'} \leq \biggl(\,\sup_{p\in\Gamma} \| S_{\bar f^{-1}(p)}\bar f \|\biggr) (1-\eps)^{-1}(1+\eps)^{r'} < 1\quad \forall\,r'=0,\ldots,r\end{equation} if $\eps$ is small enough.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Identifying $N_{(\phi,z)}\Gamma\cong S_{(\phi,z) :=\mathspan\{\pa_z\}$ and the norms $\|v\pa_\phi\|=|v|$, $\|w\pa_z\|=|w|$, we indeed have~\eqref{EqINormHyp .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The desired $A_p$ is a scalar, and~\eqref{EqIEllp2 reads $A'_{\bar f^{-1 (\phi,0) =1^{-1}\cdot(A_{(\phi,0)}\cdot\frac12+1)$ with fixed point $A\equiv 2$; and indeed $\bar N^s=\mathspan\{ 2\pa_\phi+\pa_z \}$ is invariant under $D\bar f$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: We proceed to discuss the first condition in~\eqref{EqINormHyp in this context in some generality.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Let $M$ be a smooth manifold, and let $X\subset M$ a smooth compact submanifold.
elements.sent-2411-053: Write $\phi_V^t$ for the time $t$ flow of $V$, and $N_x\phi_V^t\colon N_x X\to N_{\phi_V^t(x) X$ for the map induced by $D_x\phi_V^t\colon T_x M\to T_{\phi_V^t(x) M$ on the normal bundle.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Write $\cI\subset\CI(M)$ for the ideal of functions vanishing on $X$.
elements.sent-2411-055: Define the linearization of $V$ at $X$ by \begin{equation}\label{EqULin} L_V \in \Diff^1(X;N^*X),\quad L_V\colon\dd f\mapsto\dd(V f)\ \ (f\in\cI).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-056: Since $\CI(X;N^*X)\cong\cI/\cI^2$ via $\dd f\mapsfrom f+\cI^2$, $f\in\cI$, and since $V\colon\cI^j\to\cI^j$, $j=1,2$, this is well-defined.
elements.sent-2411-057: Given a local frame $\dd f_1,\ldots,\dd f_k$ of $N^*X$ over $U\subset X$, we thus have (summation convention) \begin{equation}\label{EqULinExpl} L_V(g^i\,\dd f_i) = (V g^j + \ell^j{}_i g^i)\dd f_j,\quad g_i\in\CI(U),\quad L_V(\dd f_i) =: \ell^j{}_i\dd f_j,\ \ell^i{}_j\in\CI(U).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}\label{EqULinExpl} L_V(g^i\,\dd f_i) = (V g^j + \ell^j{}_i g^i)\dd f_j,\quad g_i\in\CI(U),\quad L_V(\dd f_i) =: \ell^j{}_i\dd f_j,\ \ell^i{}_j\in\CI(U).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-058: Therefore, $L_V$ induces a partial connection on $N^*X$ along integral curves of $V$ in $X$, and therefore a notion of parallel transport $P_V^t\colon N^*_x X\to N^*_{\phi_V^t(x) X$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: We begin with a preliminary computation at $x$.
elements.sent-2411-060: For $f\in\cI$, we compute \begin{equation}\frac{\dd}{\dd t}(\dd f\circ N_x\phi_V^t)\Big|_{t=0} = \frac{\dd}{\dd t}(\dd f\circ D_x\phi_V^t)\Big|_{t=0} = \frac{\dd}{\dd t}\bigl((\phi_V^t)^*(\dd f)\bigr)\Big|_{t=0} = \cL_V(\dd f)=\dd(V f) = L_V(f).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}\frac{\dd}{\dd t}(\dd f\circ N_x\phi_V^t)\Big|_{t=0} = \frac{\dd}{\dd t}(\dd f\circ D_x\phi_V^t)\Big|_{t=0} = \frac{\dd}{\dd t}\bigl((\phi_V^t)^*(\dd f)\bigr)\Big|_{t=0} = \cL_V(\dd f)=\dd(V f) = L_V(f).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-061: More generally, if $f\in\cI$, $g\in\CI(M)$, then $(V f)|_X=0$ implies \begin{equation}\frac{\dd}{\dd t}(g\,\dd f\circ N_x\phi_V^t)\Big|_{t=0} = \dd(i_V(g\,\dd f)) + i_V\dd(g\,\dd f) = g L_V(f) + (V g)\dd f = L_V(g\,\dd f)\end{equation} by the Leibniz rule for $L_V$.
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation}\frac{\dd}{\dd t}(g\,\dd f\circ N_x\phi_V^t)\Big|_{t=0} = \dd(i_V(g\,\dd f)) + i_V\dd(g\,\dd f) = g L_V(f) + (V g)\dd f = L_V(g\,\dd f)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-063: We apply this to $(\phi^{t_0 _V)^*\xi$ where $t_0\in\R$ and observe $L_V\bigl((\phi_V^t)^*\xi\bigr) = \dd\la(\phi_V^t)^*\xi,V\ra = \dd\bigl( (\phi_V^t)^* \la \xi,D_x\phi_V^t(V)\ra \bigr) = (\phi_V^t)^*\dd\la\xi,V\ra = (\phi_V^t)^*L_V\xi$ to get \begin{equation}\label{EqULinIdent} \frac{\dd}{\dd t}(\xi\circ N_x\phi_V^t) = (\phi_V^t)^*L_V\xi.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Let now $n_0\in N_x X$ and $\xi_0\in N^*_x X$.
elements.sent-2411-065: For $\xi:=P_V^t(\xi_0)\in N_{\phi_V^t(x) X$, we then have \begin{equation}\frac{\dd}{\dd t}\la P_V^t(\xi_0),N_x\phi_V^t(n_0)\ra = \frac{\dd}{\dd t} (\xi\circ N_x\phi_V^t)(n_0) = 0\end{equation} by~\eqref{EqULinIdent .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Therefore, $\la\xi_0,n_0\ra=\la P_V^t(\xi_0),N_x\phi_V^t(n_0)\ra$ for all $t$, as was to be shown.
elements.sent-2411-068: Take dual inner products on $N X$ and $N^*X$.
elements.sent-2411-069: We consider Kerr--Newman(--de~Sitter or --anti de~Sitter) spacetimes with mass $\bhm\geq 0$, angular momentum $a$, charge $Q$, and cosmological constant $\Lambda\in\R$.
elements.sent-2411-070: We consider a value $r_+$ of the standard coordinate $r$ at which there is a non-degenerate horizon.
elements.sent-2411-071: Special cases include subextremal Kerr ($\Lambda=0$, and $r_+=\bhm+\sqrt{\bhm^2-a^2}$ is the radius of the event horizon), de~Sitter space ($\bhm=0$, $\Lambda>0$, and $r_+=\sqrt{3/\Lambda}$ is the radius of the cosmological horizon), and subextremal Kerr--(Newman--)de~Sitter ($\Lambda>0$, and $r_+$ is the radius of either the event or the cosmological horizon).
elements.sent-2411-072: In Boyer--Lindquist coordinates, the metric (see e.g.\ \cite[\S{A ]{PodolskyGriffithsKerrNewmanAcc ) is \begin{equation}g_0 = -\frac{\mu}{b^2\varrho^2}(\dd t-a\sin^2\theta\,\dd\phi)^2 + \varrho^2\Bigl(\frac{\dd r^2}{\mu}+\frac{\dd\theta^2}{\varkappa}\Bigr) + \frac{\varkappa\sin^2\theta}{b^2\varrho^2}(a\,\dd t-(r^2+a^2)\,\dd\phi)^2, b=1+\frac{\Lambda a^2}{3},\ \varkappa=1+\frac{\Lambda a^2}{3}\cos^2\theta,\ \varrho^2=r^2+a^2\cos^2\theta, \ \mu=(r^2+a^2)\Bigl(1-\frac{\Lambda r^2}{3}\Bigr) - 2\bhm r+b^2 Q^2.\end{equation}. We require $b>0$.}}
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation}g_0 = -\frac{\mu}{b^2\varrho^2}(\dd t-a\sin^2\theta\,\dd\phi)^2 + \varrho^2\Bigl(\frac{\dd r^2}{\mu}+\frac{\dd\theta^2}{\varkappa}\Bigr) + \frac{\varkappa\sin^2\theta}{b^2\varrho^2}(a\,\dd t-(r^2+a^2)\,\dd\phi)^2, b=1+\frac{\Lambda a^2}{3},\ \varkappa=1+\frac{\Lambda a^2}{3}\cos^2\theta,\ \varrho^2=r^2+a^2\cos^2\theta, \ \mu=(r^2+a^2)\Bigl(1-\frac{\Lambda r^2}{3}\Bigr) - 2\bhm r+b^2 Q^2.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-073: We consider a value $r_+$ where $\mu(r_+)=0$ and $\mu'(r_+)>0$.
elements.sent-2411-074: These conditions are valid at the event horizon.\footnote{One can equally well consider the case $\mu'(r_+)<0$ which occurs at the cosmological horizon of a de~Sitter type spacetime; we leave it to the reader to track the sign changes in the arguments below.}
elements.sent-2411-075: We pass to Kerr star type coordinates $t_*,r,\theta,\phi_*$ regular near $r=r_+$, \begin{equation}\dd t=\dd t_*-\frac{b(r^2+a^2)}{\mu}\,\dd r,\quad \dd\phi=\dd\phi_*-\frac{b a}{\mu}\,\dd r.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-076: Writing covectors as $\zeta=\sigma\,\dd t_*+\xi\,\dd r+\eta_\theta\,\dd\theta+\eta_\phi\,\dd\phi_*$, we then compute for $G_0(\zeta)=g_0^{-1 (\zeta,\zeta)$: \begin{equation}\label{EqHG0} \sfG_0 := \varrho^2 G_0 = \mu\xi^2 + 2 b\bigl( (r^2+a^2)\sigma + a\eta_\phi \bigr)\xi + \sC_0,\quad \sC_0 := \varkappa\eta_\theta^2 + \frac{b^2}{\varkappa\sin^2\theta}(\eta_\phi+a\sin^2\theta\,\sigma)^2.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Since $\varrho^2 g_0^{-1 (\dd r,\dd r)=\mu=0$ at $r=r_+$, the hypersurface \begin{equation}\label{EqH0} \cH_0 := \R_{t_*} \times \Sph^2_{\theta,\phi_*} \times \{r_+\} \subset M = \R_{t_*}\times\Sph^2_{\theta,\phi_*} \times (r_+-2\delta,r_++2\delta)\end{equation} is a null hypersurface (the event horizon for $g_0$), and $G_0$ vanishes on $N^*\cH_0$, in particular on \begin{equation}N_-^*\cH_0=\{(t_*,r,\theta,\phi_*,\sigma,\xi,\eta_\theta,\eta_\phi)\colon r=r_+,\ \sigma=\eta_\theta=\eta_\phi=0,\ \xi<0\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-078: We consider here only the half $\xi<0$ of $N^*\cH_0\setminus o$ since $H_{\sfG_0 t_*=\pa_\sigma\sfG_0=2 b(r_+^2+a^2)\xi<0$ there.}
elements.sent-2411-079: It will be convenient to work on the spherical cotangent bundle $S^*M=(T^*M\setminus o)/\R_+$ near $S N_-^*\cH_0$ (the image of $N_-^*\cH_0$ in $S^*M$) by introducing the fiber coordinates \begin{equation}\hat\sigma=\frac{\sigma}{\xi},\ \hat\eta_\theta=\frac{\eta_\theta}{\xi},\ \hat\eta_\phi=\frac{\eta_\phi}{\xi}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-080: For now, we keep track also of the inverse fiber-radial coordinate $\rho_\infty=-\frac{1 {\xi >0$.}}

elements.sent-2411-187: Applying this for $z\in E$ and a future causal curve $\gamma\colon[0,\bar s)\to M$, with $\gamma(0)=z$ and $\bar r=\limsup_{s\to\bar s r(\gamma(s))>r_+$, to $z'=\gamma(s)$ where $t_*(\gamma(s))$ is large and $r(\gamma(s))\geq r':=\frac{r_++\bar r {2}$, we conclude that $z$ can be connected to $\scri^+$ by a causal curve.}}
elements.sent-2411-189: This implies that $\cH$ is the boundary of $J^-(\scri^+)$ in $\{t_*\geq t_0\}$, and $\cB=B$, $\cE=E$.
